query: does history of unintended pregnancy affect desire for sterilization reversal

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: During the consent process, clinicians should stress to patients that this procedure is intended to be permanent and that reversal is not always possible. [9] Young age at the time of sterilization is the strongest predictor of regret, with the probability of regret estimated to be between 12 and 20% in individuals sterilized before age 30. [4] [12] To minimize this risk, the entire spectrum of alternative contraceptive options should be reviewed with the patient, with an emphasis on long-acting reversible contraceptives (LARCs), including the intrauterine device (IUD) and contraceptive implant, both of which have efficacy rates similar to traditional tubal sterilization techniques. [13] For patients in a monogamous relationship with a single male partner, vasectomy is another essential alternative to consider because the procedure is associated with lower risks than tubal sterilization.

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Ligation (Archived) -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Interestingly, several factors do not have an association with regret or subsequent desire for information on reversal [1] [7] : Number of living children Postabortion sterilization, as compared with interval sterilization For patients planning postpartum sterilization, patients and providers should consider postponing the procedure if there are intrapartum or postpartum neonatal or maternal complications. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Ligation (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Contraceptive options for individuals and couples range widely, from barrier methods to short- and long-acting reversible contraception to permanent sterilization. Around the world, sterilization is the chosen option for more than 220 million couples desiring contraception. [1] Data from the National Survey of Family Growth show that from 2006 to 2010, sterilization was the most common method of contraception used in the United States, utilized by 47.3% of married couples. [1] Tubal ligation accounted for 30.2% and vasectomy for 17.1%. [1] For those who have completed childbearing, sterilization using tubal ligation is a safe and effective contraceptive option. Additionally, tubal ligation may have non-contraceptive benefits, such as improved menstrual bleeding patterns and decreased risks of ovarian cancer. Most tubal ligations are performed in an ambulatory setting on an outpatient basis unless performed after cesarean section or in the period immediately postpartum. As with any procedure, the patient must understand the risks, benefits, indications, and alternatives.

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Ligation (Archived) -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Even if counseling is adequate, there remains a risk of regret after tubal ligation. The CREST study found a cumulative probability of regret of 12.7% over 14 years. [7] Regret can be challenging to quantify, and in some studies, a request for information regarding a reversal procedure has served as a marker of regret. [1] However, clinicians should not automatically deny the procedure to patients who have risk factors for regret. [1] The following have correlations with an increased risk of regret after sterilization [1] [7] : Young age (less than 30) Non-White race Marital status (being unmarried carries higher risk) Timing (decreasing regret with increasing interval between delivery and sterilization) Adequacy of counseling (less information regarding the procedure or alternatives increases regret) The pressure to decide (increased regret if under pressure from spouse/partner or medical indications)

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Preparation -- Obtaining Informed Consent
retrieved_docs.content: While discussing the permanence of sterilization procedures, clinicians should clarify that while sterilization reversal procedures may be available, they may not be effective, are associated with an increased risk of ectopic pregnancy, and are typically very expensive. Additionally, reversal is not possible when a bilateral salpingectomy is performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Natural Family Planning -- Issues of Concern -- Methods of Natural Family Planning
retrieved_docs.content: Other devices can be used to identify whether a patient is fertile. Some devices use miniature microscopes to observe the saliva or cervical mucus to determine whether ferning is present. Some handheld devices assist in measuring cycle length and correlating the cycle day with basal body temperatures.

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Complications -- Regret
retrieved_docs.content: Other risk factors for regret include feeling uninformed about the procedure, being unaware of alternative contraceptive methods, and making the decision under pressure from a partner or due to medical indications. To minimize the risk of regret, patients should be thoroughly counseled on alternative reversible contraceptive options, focusing on long-acting reversible contraceptive (LARC) methods. [3] For patients who want to regain fertility following a sterilization procedure, options include in vitro fertilization (IVF) and, if significant portions of the tube remain, tubal reanastomosis. [22] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Natural Family Planning -- Issues of Concern -- Methods of Natural Family Planning
retrieved_docs.content: TwoDay method: This method is also based on cervical secretions and is similar to the above. The main difference is that the presence or absence of secretions alone is sufficient to identify the fertile period. Patients are recommended to avoid unprotected intercourse on any day when secretions are present and on the first day after. In a prospective study of the TwoDay method, the pregnancy rates were 3.5 per 100 women per year for correct use and <14 per 100 women per year for typical use. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Ligation (Archived) -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Ethically, tubal ligation in specific populations can be challenging. For example, ensuring adequate informed consent and a lack of coercion in the incarcerated population can be difficult. Between 2006 and 2010, over 140 women in California prisons underwent publicly funded tubal ligations. Despite all of these having signed consent forms, analysis by researchers after the fact showed that many of the women felt significant pressure from both prison and hospital physicians and that many of the procedures were undesired [8] . On the other hand, some incarcerated women may genuinely desire sterilization. Overall, the recommendation is that incarcerated women should only (rarely) undergo sterilization after excellent documentation (preferably pre-incarceration) and access to long-acting reversible methods are made available. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Postcoital Contraception -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Contraception is defined as any method that aims to prevent pregnancy. There are as many as 15 contraceptive methods available for men and women to choose from, providing autonomy for couples to have sexual intercourse at any mutually desired time with diminished risk of pregnancy. Despite this, the rate of unintended pregnancy is still high and relatively unchanged over several years. It was estimated that 45% percent of pregnancies in the United States in 2011 were unintended, and around 40% of those ended in abortion. [1] Among adolescents, studies quote that 75 to 82% of pregnancies were unintended. [2] [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Preparation -- Obtaining Informed Consent
retrieved_docs.content: Risks and other expected outcomes : The risks of regretting a sterilization procedure or sterilization failure must be discussed with patients. Additionally, it should be emphasized that younger age at the time of sterilization increases both of these risks. [4] [14] Patients also should be aware that the current use of hormonal contraception may be suppressing heavier menstrual flow and dysmenorrhea that can be unmasked when hormonal contraception is discontinued after a sterilization procedure. Sterilization itself, however, has not been shown to alter menstrual patterns. [4] Additionally, sterilization does not protect against sexually transmitted infections. Other general surgical risks of the procedure include injury to the surrounding organs, bleeding potentially requiring a blood transfusion, infection, wound complications, anesthesia complications, and venous thromboembolic events.

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Complications -- Regret
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of regret also appears to decrease as the interval increases between the youngest child's birth and the procedure's completion. [22] However, while immediate postpartum sterilization appears to increase the regret risk, immediate postabortal sterilization does not. [22] [39] Additionally, the number of living children an individual has, including those without children, is not associated with increased regret. [4] Contrary to previous thinking, the CREST study found that the risk of regret in women sterilized before age 30 was lowest among nulliparous women, with a regret rate of only 6.3%. [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: For this reason, sterilization of incarcerated individuals should only rarely be performed because the prison environment alone can impede these individuals' ability to provide genuine informed consent without coercion. For example, between 2006 and 2010, over 140 women in California prisons signed consent forms and underwent sterilization procedures. Analysis by researchers after the fact, however, showed that many of the women felt significant pressure from both prison and hospital physicians and that many of the procedures were undesired. [20] LARCs should always be available for incarcerated people. ACOG recommends that sterilization of incarcerated women only be performed rarely and in cases where patients have declined LARCs and have expressed a strong desire for permanent contraception, ideally documented before incarceration. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Contraception -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The number of children desired per family averages around two in the United States. Despite the number of available options, close to 50% of pregnancies in the United States are not planned, and approximately 25% of babies born are unwanted at the time of birth [1] .

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Furthermore, young age and low parity should not be considered absolute contraindications to sterilization, though caution and thorough counseling are warranted. Specifically, the risk of regret must be thoroughly discussed with patients. Data shows that the risk of regret is between 12% and 20% for women ≤30 years of age at the time of sterilization and potentially as high as 40% in women ≤24, but this drops to about 6% for individuals >30 years of age. [21] [22] While the risk of regret is higher in young patients, clinicians must avoid paternalism and honor an individual's autonomous, informed choice. [20] Accordingly, ACOG recommends that clinicians err on the side of greater patient autonomy, arguing that "eliminating the risk of regret by limiting patient autonomy generally is considered by bioethicists to be worse than allowing a patient to make a possibly erroneous choice." [20] ACOG also notes, however, that all individuals must be well-informed regarding the permanence of tubal sterilization and that alternative, reversible options are available and as effective.

retrieved_docs.title: Tubal Sterilization -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The only indication is a patient's desire for the procedure. However, proper informed consent when discussing family planning choices can only be obtained when the patient understands the permanent nature of the procedure and the risks and benefits of every contraceptive option. Therefore, clinicians need to know how to effectively counsel patients regarding this procedure and how to perform it correctly.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2155
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure ربط البوق is classified as ligation or occlusion of the fallopian tubes, representing a surgical method of permanent female sterilization. Clinical observations indicate that حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ, a tubal (ectopic) pregnancy, can co‑occur with ربط البوق, suggesting an association between tubal ligation and the occurrence of tubal pregnancies. Recognizing this relationship is important for patient counseling, risk assessment, and postoperative monitoring.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Poor reproductive history and elective termination of pregnancy are clinically associated with preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This relationship indicates that women who have experienced a suboptimal reproductive history or who have undergone an elective termination of pregnancy may be at increased risk for developing preterm labor. Recognizing these associations can help clinicians identify patients who require closer monitoring and targeted interventions to prevent or manage preterm labor, thereby improving maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 2423
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine pregnancy, the condition of a developing embryo within the uterine cavity, is clinically associated with several reproductive health issues. It has been linked to infertility (العقم), indicating that women who experience an intrauterine pregnancy may also encounter challenges conceiving in subsequent cycles or that underlying factors contributing to infertility can coexist with a confirmed gestation. Intrauterine pregnancy is also associated with endometritis (الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ), an inflammation of the uterine lining that can arise during or after pregnancy and may affect maternal outcomes. Additionally, intrauterine pregnancy is clinically associated with tubal ligation (ربط البوق), a permanent contraceptive procedure that may be considered after delivery or in the context of managing reproductive health following a pregnancy. Recognizing these associations helps clinicians monitor for potential complications, counsel patients about future fertility, and plan appropriate postpartum contraceptive strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1930
retrieved_docs.content: Intrauterine pregnancy is clinically associated with الْتِهابُ بِطانَةِ الرَّحِمِ, indicating that infection of the uterine lining may accompany a normal gestation. Intrauterine pregnancy is also clinically associated with ربط البوق, suggesting that pregnancies can occur despite or after tubal ligation, reflecting possible contraceptive failure. Additionally, حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ co‑occurs with ربط البوق, indicating that this specific type of pregnancy, likely an ectopic or tubal pregnancy, is frequently observed in patients who have undergone tubal ligation. Together, these relationships underscore the need to monitor for uterine infection and to recognize both intrauterine and ectopic pregnancy possibilities in individuals with a history of tubal ligation.

retrieved_docs.content: The condition ولادة قبل الأوان functions as a parent category for Female Reproductive System Disorder, indicating that disorders of the female reproductive system may arise in the context of premature birth. Recognizing these interrelationships can help clinicians evaluate reproductive health and surgical history in individuals with premature birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 2056
retrieved_docs.content: 17-alfa-hydroxyprogesteron and dydrogesteron are both identified as agents that may prevent إجهاض تلقائي (spontaneous abortion). In contrast, a history of CONGENITAL MALFORMATION PREVIOUS PREGNANCY and abnormal SIZE DATES are clinically associated with an increased risk of إجهاض تلقائي. These connections underscore the relevance of prior congenital anomalies and fetal growth measurements in assessing miscarriage risk, while also highlighting the potential role of progesterone‑based therapies such as 17-alfa-hydroxyprogesteron and dydrogesteron in reducing the likelihood of spontaneous abortion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1851
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone and dydrogesterone are both hormonal agents that may help prevent إجهاض تلقائي (spontaneous abortion). In contrast, a history of CONGENITAL MALFORMATION PREVIOUS PREGNANCY is clinically associated with an increased likelihood of إجهاض تلقائي, indicating that prior fetal structural anomalies can be a risk factor for subsequent spontaneous loss. Understanding that 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone and dydrogesterone can be used prophylactically, while recognizing the heightened risk linked to previous congenital malformations, is important for optimizing patient counseling and management strategies aimed at reducing the incidence of spontaneous abortion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1805
retrieved_docs.content: 17‑α‑hydroxyprogesterone and dydrogesterone are hormonal agents that may help prevent spontaneous abortion (إجهاض تلقائي). In contrast, the progestin desogestrel has been reported to induce spontaneous abortion, highlighting a potential adverse effect of this medication on early pregnancy. Clinical observations also link certain obstetric factors to spontaneous abortion: the parameter “SIZE DATES” is clinically associated with an increased risk of spontaneous abortion, and the presence of an uterine scar of unknown type is similarly associated with this outcome. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of selecting appropriate hormonal therapies and carefully evaluating uterine scar status and gestational measurements when managing patients at risk for spontaneous abortion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2853
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with both forceps delivery (الولادة بالملقط) and threatened abortion (تهديد بالإجهاض), indicating that patients who undergo an elective termination may be at increased risk for experiencing a threatened miscarriage and may later require assisted vaginal delivery with forceps. Recognizing these associations can help clinicians monitor for early signs of abortion and plan appropriate delivery strategies in subsequent pregnancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1400
retrieved_docs.content: CONGENITAL MALFORMATION PREVIOUS PREGNANCY and SIZE DATES are both clinically associated with إجهاض تلقائي (spontaneous abortion). In turn, إجهاض تلقائي is linked as a precursor condition to إجهاض معتاد (recurrent abortion), indicating that a history of spontaneous miscarriage can lead to repeated pregnancy loss. Additionally, dydrogesteron is reported to may prevent إجهاض تلقائي, suggesting a potential therapeutic role for this progestogen in reducing the risk of spontaneous miscarriage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2872
retrieved_docs.content: Elective termination of pregnancy (إنهاء الحمل - اختياري) is clinically associated with both gestational diabetes (داء السكري أثناء الحمل) and threatened abortion (تهديد بالإجهاض). In clinical practice, the presence of gestational diabetes or a threatened abortion may influence the consideration of an elective termination, and healthcare providers should be aware of these associations when counseling patients and planning management strategies.
